context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: State

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of the State in development planning? Is the 'Third World State' expected to behave differently from the 'First World' State in its relation to industry, private sector, financial markets and social programs? What is the impact of globalization on the State? What distinguishes a State from other actors in planning?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m trying to figure out this reflection on the role of the State in development planning. I’m a little unsure how to separate the different parts of the prompt, especially the “Third World vs First World State” part and globalization. Could you help me break it down?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — the prompt does have several moving parts. Before we break it down, tell me: in your own words, what do you think the State's basic role in development planning is? What comes to mind first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help breaking down the prompt. Rather than structuring it for them, I should use the Socratic method to get them to engage with the material first. I'll ask them what they already think about the core question to activate prior knowledge and let them lead the initial reasoning. I want to keep it bite-sized and not over-scaffold.

exchanges.student: I think the State is kind of the main actor because it makes rules and decides priorities, like where money goes or what gets built. I’m not totally sure if that’s too broad, but I guess its role is to guide development and coordinate other actors.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid starting intuition. Now push it a bit further: lots of actors guide development — NGOs, international organizations, private firms. What makes the State *different* from all of them? What does it have that others don't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a reasonable starting answer that touches on key ideas like rule-making and coordination. I should affirm the direction without validating it as complete, and push them to think more critically about what makes the State *unique* compared to other actors — which is directly addressed in the prompt. This moves the conversation forward toward the solution.

exchanges.student: I think the State has authority that the others don’t, like it can actually make laws and enforce them. NGOs or firms can influence things, but they can’t really set the rules for everyone. Is “legitimacy” also part of the difference, or am I mixing that up?
exchanges.tutor: You're not mixing it up at all — legitimacy is very relevant here. But what do you think legitimacy actually *means* in this context? Why would it matter that the State's authority is seen as legitimate, rather than just powerful?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correctly identifying coercive authority and rule-making as distinguishing features, and is now probing the concept of legitimacy on their own. This is great progress and directly relevant to the prompt and course themes. I should affirm the direction and push them to think about what legitimacy actually means in this context, rather than confirming it outright.

exchanges.student: I think legitimacy means people recognize the State as having a right to govern, not just the ability to force people. That probably matters because planning needs compliance and acceptance, not only coercion. I’m still a little fuzzy on how to say that in a development-planning way.
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning. Now connect it to the assignment: do you think a 'Third World State' has the same level of legitimacy and capacity to enforce rules as a 'First World State'? What historical or political factors might make a difference there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has grasped the legitimacy concept well and is now asking for help articulating it in a development planning context. Per the rules, I should not write or suggest language for them. Instead, I'll push them to connect this idea to the specific context of developing countries, which moves toward the 'Third World State' part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I don’t think it’s automatically the same, because a lot of postcolonial states may have weaker institutions or contested legitimacy. Maybe colonial legacies, dependence on aid, internal conflict, or pressure from outside actors can make state capacity less stable. I feel like I’m generalizing too much though.
exchanges.tutor: Generalizing is fine for a reflection, as long as you acknowledge it. You've identified real pressures on Third World states — now think about globalization specifically. How do you think it affects a state's ability to plan and make autonomous decisions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making strong connections to the course themes (colonialism, external actors, institutional capacity) but is self-doubting due to fear of overgeneralizing. This is actually a productive tension for a reflection piece. I should validate the direction and push them to think about what the implications are for development planning specifically — moving toward the globalization part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I guess globalization reduces autonomy because states have to respond to global markets, investors, trade rules, and institutions like the IMF or World Bank. So even if a state wants to plan in a certain way, it might be constrained by capital flight or loan conditions. I think that makes sense, but I’m not sure if globalization only weakens the state or can also change its role.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation — you've caught something important. What might a *transformed* state role look like under globalization? Could the state gain anything, or take on new functions, even as it loses some autonomy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is thinking critically and has arrived at a nuanced observation — that globalization might not only weaken the state but could also transform its role. This is excellent thinking and directly relevant to the prompt. I should affirm this and push them to develop that nuance further, as it will strengthen their reflection piece.

exchanges.student: Maybe the State shifts from directly controlling development to regulating, negotiating, and managing connections to global markets. So it might lose some freedom, but still matter a lot because it sets the terms for investment, labor standards, or social protection. That clicked a bit — I just don’t know how to turn it into a clear structure for the reflection.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually built up all the key ideas you need. Before thinking about structure, try listing the 3-4 main points you want to make in the reflection. What would those be, in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now covered all the main components of the prompt through our discussion: the State's unique role, legitimacy, Third World vs First World differences, and globalization's impact. They're now asking for help structuring their reflection. Per the rules, I cannot write or suggest language, but I can prompt them to think about how to organize their own ideas. We've had about 7 exchanges, so I should start nudging them toward actually writing. I'll ask them to identify the main ideas they want to hit, which is a step toward drafting without me doing the work.

exchanges.student:
Okay, maybe:  
1. The State is distinct because it has legitimate authority to make and enforce rules.  
2. In development planning, it coordinates resources, institutions, and other actors.  
3. “Third World” states are often expected to do more direct intervention but may face weaker capacity and more external pressure.  
4. Globalization doesn’t erase the State, but changes its role into more of a mediator/regulator.  

I think point 3 is still messy.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — point 3 is doing two different things at once. Can you separate the *expectation* placed on Third World states from the *reality* of their capacity? What creates that gap?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid outline covering the main prompt components. Point 3 is indeed underdeveloped — it conflates two different ideas: the expectation of what Third World states *should* do and the reality of what they *can* do given constraints. I should push them to untangle this without telling them how, as it will strengthen their reflection. This is a good moment to push toward drafting.

exchanges.student: I think the expectation is that they intervene more directly—like building industry, steering finance, and expanding basic social programs—because markets are weaker or less inclusive. But the reality is they may have less capacity and legitimacy to do that effectively, partly because of colonial legacies, limited resources, debt, conflict, and outside pressure. Is that the kind of gap the prompt wants me to talk about?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's precisely the kind of tension the prompt is pointing at. You now have all the pieces. I'd encourage you to start drafting — even a rough first attempt. What would your opening sentence be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has clearly articulated the expectation vs. reality gap in point 3, which is exactly the kind of nuance the prompt is looking for. They've now covered all components of the prompt well. This is a good moment to push them to start writing their reflection rather than continuing to discuss, as we're approaching the planned conversation length and there's a risk of over-discussing without producing text.

exchanges.student:
Maybe: “The State plays a central role in development planning because it is the only actor with legitimate authority to set rules, allocate public resources, and coordinate social and economic change.”  

Does that sound too formal, or is it okay for this kind of reflection?
exchanges.tutor: That's a good start — it captures the core idea. Don't get stuck polishing the opening; keep drafting. What comes next, based on your outline?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate or polish their writing, which I should not do per the rules. However, I can give a general evaluative comment and redirect them to keep writing rather than seeking approval at every step. I should not suggest language or edits, but I can affirm the direction and push them forward.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout all ten turns. Every key idea — legitimacy, state capacity, colonial legacies, globalization's transformative effect — was drawn out through targeted questions rather than stated by the tutor. The student arrived at all four outline points independently. Scaffolding was well-sequenced, building from basic intuition to nuanced distinctions. Responses were concise and appropriately bite-sized. Tone was consistently supportive without being evaluative. No submission-ready content was provided by the tutor.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted across any criterion. The tutor never gave the student a conclusion before the student reasoned toward it; each turn posed a focused follow-up question that advanced the student's own thinking. Scaffolding progressed logically from defining the state's role, to legitimacy, to Third World/First World distinctions, to globalization, to structuring the reflection. Responses were short and interactive throughout, avoiding monologue. Tone was warm and formative, with no grading language. The conversation stayed tightly anchored to the assignment prompt at all times.
